coredata.dc:title: Oxidant-assisted preparation of carbon dots pairs for construction of a dual-channel fluorescence sensor array for tetracyclines discrimination
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jing
Li, Su
Duan, Ya-Lan
Song, Yan-Ting
Li, Lu-Shuang
coredata.dc:description: The improper or excessive use of tetracyclines (TCs) can lead to residues in the environment and food, posing a global environmental concern. It is imperative to explore simple and economical methods for detecting and discriminating multiple TCs simultaneously. In this work, oxidant-doping carbon dots (OCDs) and reference carbon dots without oxidant-doping (RCDs) were synthesized in the presence and absence of ammonium persulphate (APS), resulting in distinct compositions of functional groups. TCs can quench the fluorescence of the two CDs to different extents, thus a two-channel fluorescence sensor array for five kinds of TCs was constructed. The discrepancies in fluorescence response of the two CDs towards various TCs were maximized through regulating the spiked concentration of APS. Under optimal sensing conditions, each of the five TCs produced unique fluorescence response patterns, allowing for differentiation within concentration ranges of 0.5 ∼ 50 μg mL−1 based on linear discriminant analysis. Discrimination of binary, ternary and quaternary mixtures of TCs was achieved through the sensor array. The sensor array was successfully applied to tap water, lake water, and milk samples, yielding satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 85.1 % to 114.4 %. Additionally, molecular docking was initially employed to investigate the interaction modes and strengths between different TCs and the CDs. Overall, this study provides a viable detection platform for multiple TCs, demonstrating significant potential for environmental monitoring and food quality control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor array
Tetracyclines
Oxidant-assisted
Linear discriminant analysis
Molecular docking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Discrimination of TCs with sensor array based on RCDs/OCDs
Discrimination of TCs in real samples
Selectivity and discernibility of the sensor array to TCs
Discrimination and quantification of TCs in real samples
The mechanism of the sensor array for identification of TCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the scientific research project of Xiangyang Central Hospital (2024YJ04A) and the Research Fund from the Collaborative Innovation Center of One Health of Hainan University (XTCX2022JKB05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of RCDs and OCDs. TEM images of RCDs (a) and OCDs (b). (Inset: HRTEM images of individual CDs). FTIR (c) and XPS full-scan survey (d) spectra of RCDs and OCDs. High-resolution C 1 s (e), O 1 s (f), N 1 s (g) and S 2p (h) spectra of RCDs and OCDs.

Response of the sensor array to TCs and other chemicals. (a-b) fluorescence spectra of two CDs in the absence and presence of different TCs (10 μg mL−1); (c) quenching efficiency (F0/F) of the sensor array in response to TCs and other chemicals at a concentration of 10 μg mL−1; (d) a heat map depicting the fingerprint-like fluorescence response pattern of the CDs to TCs at 10 μg mL−1; (e-f) canonical score plots for the discrimination of five TCs at different concentrations (e: 10 μg mL−1; f: 0.5 μg mL−1; g: 50 μg mL−1).
Canonical score plots for the discrimination of TCs at different concentrations (a-e) and responding linear plots of the factor 1 versus the concentration of TCs (f-j).

Application performance of the sensor array in discrimination TCs mixtures and in real samples. (a-c) LDA plots for discrimination of binary, ternary and quaternary TCs mixtures at identical percentages; (d-f) LDA plots for discrimination of three representative binary mixtures (OTC + TET; CTC + OTC; TET + DOX) with different mass ratios; (g-i) LDA plots for analysis of TCs in lake water, tap water and milk samples.

Exploration of sensing mechanisms. Comparison of the absorption spectra of TCs and the fluorescence spectra of RCDs (a) and OCDs (b). Time-resolved decay curves RCDs (c) and OCDs (d) with and without TCs. (Inset: fitted lines of decay curves with the double exponential decay function.) Molecular docking study between functional groups of RCDs (e)/OCDs (f) and CTC. (Grey part for graphite carbon core; the green structures are citric acid and DSS; the red structure is APS; the structures of the blue are CTC. The orange dashed line indicates the cation-π interaction; the yellow dotted line indicates hydrogen bonding; the cyan dashed line indicates the π-π action. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of the developed fluorescence sensor array with the reported ones for TCs discrimination.

row.entry: FL + UV–vis spectra + FL lifetime + ζ potential
FL + UV–vis spectra + FL lifetime + molecular docking

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *All the units have been converted into μM for ease of comparison with other reports.
The signal of / only means not involved in the article.

head.ce:title: Oxidant-assisted preparation of carbon dots pairs for construction of a dual-channel fluorescence sensor array for tetracyclines discrimination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xiangyang Central Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Affiliated Hospital of Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xiangyang Central Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Collaborative Innovation Center of One Health, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education
Collaborative Innovation Center of One Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Collaborative Innovation Center of One Health, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jing Li and Su Li contributed to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The improper or excessive use of tetracyclines (TCs) can lead to residues in the environment and food, posing a global environmental concern. It is imperative to explore simple and economical methods for detecting and discriminating multiple TCs simultaneously. In this work, oxidant-doping carbon dots (OCDs) and reference carbon dots without oxidant-doping (RCDs) were synthesized in the presence and absence of ammonium persulphate (APS), resulting in distinct compositions of functional groups. TCs can quench the fluorescence of the two CDs to different extents, thus a two-channel fluorescence sensor array for five kinds of TCs was constructed. The discrepancies in fluorescence response of the two CDs towards various TCs were maximized through regulating the spiked concentration of APS. Under optimal sensing conditions, each of the five TCs produced unique fluorescence response patterns, allowing for differentiation within concentration ranges of 0.5 ∼ 50 μg mL−1 based on linear discriminant analysis. Discrimination of binary, ternary and quaternary mixtures of TCs was achieved through the sensor array. The sensor array was successfully applied to tap water, lake water, and milk samples, yielding satisfactory recovery rates ranging from 85.1 % to 114.4 %. Additionally, molecular docking was initially employed to investigate the interaction modes and strengths between different TCs and the CDs. Overall, this study provides a viable detection platform for multiple TCs, demonstrating significant potential for environmental monitoring and food quality control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs) are a group of broad-spectrum antibiotics characterized by a common parent structure, which includes tetracycline (TET), chlortetracycline (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), doxycycline (DOX), and tigecycline (TGC), etc. They are widely utilized as feed additives to promote animal growth and prevent bacterial diseases in the livestock and aquaculture industries [1]. However, due to misuse and improper disposal, TCs persist in the environment and in animal-derived food products [2–4]. On account of the difficult degradation and serious bioaccumulation, long-term exposure to TCs can result in bacterial resistance, colony disorders, teeth discoloration, liver and kidney damage, thus posing a significant threat to human health [5,6]. Consequently, it is critical to develop effective analytical methods for TCs. Currently, traditional techniques for monitoring TCs include high performance liquid chromatography or coupled with mass spectrometry [7], capillary electrophoresis [8], and electrochemical analysis [9]. Despite their high sensitivity and accuracy, these methods are limited by their tedious operation, time consumption, and the high requirements for expensive instruments and skilled personnel [10]. Therefore, it is essential to develop efficient and economical analytical methods to identify and detect multiple TCs in various environments, including remote areas where resources are scarce.

Fluorescence sensors have garnered considerable attention due to their ease of operation, rapid response times, low cost and high sensitivity. Various fluorescence materials have been applied to construct sensors for TCs detection [10–14]. In comparison to organic fluorophores, quantum dots and metal nanoclusters, carbon dots (CDs) present viable fluorescence sensing materials, possessing merits such as stable fluorescence, low toxicity and superior biocompatibility [15]. The primary mechanism of CDs-based TCs sensors relies on the fluorescence variation of CDs induced by TCs [16–18]. However, due to the common structural parent nucleus among TCs, most sensors show similar signal responses to different TCs,which limits their ability to detect individual compounds and restricts the measurements to total content alone. Furthermore, the reliability of these sensors is easily affected by complex environmental matrices.

Sensor arrays, often referred to as the “chemical nose–tongue”, serve as efficient tools for identifying multiple similar analytes [19]. Unlike conventional sensors that demonstrate high selectivity for a single analyte, sensor arrays typically consist of a series of sensing elements that respond differently to each analyte. The resulting differentiated responses can be analyzed through multivariate statistical techniques, such as linear discriminant analysis (LDA), hierarchical clustering analysis (HCA) or principal component analysis (PCA), yielding identifiable patterns for each analyte [20]. In theory, the discrimination and quantification of TCs with subtle structural differences may be achieved through sensing arrays. Several sensor arrays based on Eu3+ [21–23], CDs [24–26] and noble metallic nanoclusters [14,27] have been developed. Whereas, the increasing number of analytes, the discrimination of multivariate mixtures, and the in-depth exploration of the response mechanism present inherent challenges.

In this work, CDs are prepared hydrothermally with citric acid and diphenylamine-4-sulfonic acid sodium salt as carbon sources. Oxidant-doped CDs (OCDs) and reference CDs without oxidant doping (RCDs) were synthesized in the presence and absence of the oxidant ammonium persulphate (APS), inspired by the literature [28]. Notably, a significant difference in the surface functional groups between the two types of CDs was observed, and five TCs were found to quench the fluorescence of the two CDs to varying extents. Given that TCs possess different substituents, the interactions between the CDs and TCs may vary due to hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic interactions, and electrostatic forces, leading to distinct quenching efficiencies. Consequently, a dual-channel sensor array based on CDs was constructed to differentiate between the individual TCs in multivariate mixtures and to quantify their concentrations. Importantly, to the best of our knowledge, molecular docking was initially employed to investigate the interaction modes and strengths of different tetracyclines with the CDs. This study presents a promising method for monitoring multiple TCs in environmental and food samples.

Citric acid, diphenylamine-4-sulfonic acid sodium salt (DSS) and ammonium persulphate (APS) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Methanol, HCl and NaOH were obtained from Xilong Science Co., Ltd (Shantou, China). Doxycycline (DOX), chlortetracycline (CTC), tetracycline (TET), oxytetracycline (OTC), tigecycline (TGC), spiramycin (SPM), chloramphenicol (CHL), amoxicillin (AM), streptomycin (STR), gentamicin (GEN), neomycin (NEO), cysteine (Cys), threonine (Thr), valine (Val), tyrosine (Tyr), isoleucine (Ile), arginine (Arg), glutamicacid (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), leucine (Leu), Al(NO3)3, ZnSO4·7H2O, NaCl, CrCl3, KCl, Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O, MgSO4, FeCl3 were supplied by Shanghai Mecklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, USA).

UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded by a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a F-4700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected by a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were measured by a Scientific Nicolet iS5 Spectrometer (Thermo, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were taken by SmartLab-9 kW X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigation was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Quantum yield (QY) was determined with quinine sulfate as reference according to previous reports [29].

RCDs and OCDs were prepared via a hydrothermal route with citric acid and DSS as precursors. Briefly, 3.28 g of citric acid and 10 mg of DSS were first dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. After addition of 10 mg of APS, the obtained mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the deep yellow solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filtration membrane and the filtrate collected was OCDs. The RCDs were prepared using the same procedure, but without APS. The obtained CDs stock solutions were stored in dark at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of TCs with sensor array based on RCDs/OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor array was constructed based on the different fluorescence responses of the two sensing elements (RCDs and OCDs) towards TCs. Stock solutions of TCs were prepared in methanol and diluted with ultrapure water. For a typical sensing procedure, 50 μL of RCDs/OCDs solution were mixed with 3.0 mL of TCs samples (0.5 μg mL−1, 10 μg mL−1 and 50 μg mL−1). After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the solution was then subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement. The fluorescence measurement of RCDs sensing channel was performed at excitation wavelength (Ex) of 350 nm and emission wavelength (Em) of 470 nm. The fluorescence measurement of OCDs sensing channel was performed at Ex of 290 nm and Em of 360 nm. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) were calculated, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of the specific CDs in the absence and presence of target, respectively. Seven replicate tests were performed for each sample. As a result, a training data matrix (2 CDs × 5 TCs × 7 repetitions) was obtained. The obtained data was processed through LDA by a software of SPSS Statistics 25. For quantitative performance evaluation, TCs standard solution series were prepared and analyzed with the sensor array. Standard curves for TCs were constructed by plotting factor 1 against the concentration of analyte. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 3δ/k, where δ is the standard deviation of the measurements and k is the gradient of the linearity. Binary, ternary and quaternary mixtures of TCs with identical or different proportions at a total concentration of 10 μg mL−1 were prepared and analyzed with the sensor array to investigate the recognition ability of the sensor array to the TCs mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water collected from the lab, lake water from Dongpo Lake and milk from the supermarket were selected as the actual samples. Water samples were filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove particulates matter. For milk samples, 10 mL of 20 % acetonitrile were mixed with 10 mL of milk and homogenized for 5 min. The mixture was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 10 min and subjected to the sensor array. For spiked recovery test, stock solutions of TCs were added to the real matrix separately, achieving 10 μg mL−1, and the samples were measured according to the procedure described above. To verify the reliability of the established fluorescence sensor array, 15 samples with random TCs (10 μg mL−1) were prepared in the three real matrixes by researcher A and conducted a blind detection by researcher B with the sensor array.

The molecular structures of citric acid, DSS, APS and the five TCs were displayed in Fig. S1. While TCs differ in their substituents, minor differences in fluorescence responses of CDs to them were not sufficient to distinguish individuals in previous reports [17,30–32]. Usually, a representative was always chosen as the model analyte, and the sensor can only be used for measuring the total amount of TCs. In this study, a binary fluorescence sensor array, composed of two CDs with a large difference in their fluorescence responses to different TCs, was proposed for the identification and detection of TCs. DSS incorporating N and S elements in the chemical structure was intended to improve the QY as well as regulate the functional groups on CDs. Preliminary experiments revealed that CDs prepared with and without APS displayed distinct fluorescence responses to TCs. In order to obtain higher QY and sensitivity as well as differentiated response to TCs, the spiked amount of APS was optimized. As revealed in Fig. S2a, QY of CDs jumped sharply (from 1.43 % to 4.64 %) with the rise of APS involvement from 0 to 10 mg, and keep steady thereafter which indicated the saturation of oxidation. The discrepant fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs prepared with various amounts of APS to five kinds of TCs were tested and expressed through F0/F. As indicated in Fig. S2b, the addition of APS affected the fluorescence response of CDs to TCs in different degree, with the greatest impact on TET and the least impact on CTC. Over all considering, the CDs prepared without and with 10 mg of APS were finally selected as the two sensing elements the sensor array, which were designated as RCDs and OCDs, respectively.

The morphology and size of the CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a–b, RCDs and OCDs were both dispersed evenly with a quasi-spherical shape. The average particle sizes of RCDs and OCDs were around 3.2 nm and 3.1 nm, respectively (Fig. S3a), which were nearly the same. According to HRTEM images (insets of Fig. 1a–b), both RCDs and OCDs had an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. There was a broad peak centered around 27° in the XRD spectra of RCDs and OCDs (Fig. S3b), which was attributed to the (002) planes in graphite carbon. The XRD and TEM results confirmed each other and indicated that CDs have graphitic carbon-like lattice crystal structures, in agreement with our previous research [29,33]. The chemical composition and functional groups of CDs were characterized through XPS and FTIR. By stacking the FTIR spectra of the two CDs, almost the same absorption peaks were observed (Fig. 1c). The adsorption peaks at 3059 cm−1, 2627 cm−1, 1710 cm−1, 1638 cm−1, 1411 cm−1, 1217 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H, C-H, C = O, C = C, C-N and C-O [24], respectively. As both CDs were prepared with the same carbon source, their surface functional group species may be identical, while their composition ratios may differ due to the addition of APS. The full survey XPS spectrum of CDs were obtained, and four main peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and S 2p were observed (Fig. 1d). According to the calculations, OCDs was composed of C 43.89 %, O 52.86 %, N 1.63 % and S 1.62 %, and RCDs was composed of C 58.43 %, O 40.24 %, N 0.95 % and S 0.38 %. In the presence of oxidants, the oxygen content of the prepared OCDs increased significantly, suggesting that the OCDs contained more oxygen-containing functional groups, possibly derived from a stronger oxidation process. Moreover, a considerable increase in the content of S and N was also observed, which may be attributed to either the direct introduction of heteroatoms from APS or the fact that the presence of the oxidant facilitated the doping of heteroatoms originating from the main carbon source.

In order to further understand the differences in the surface functional group composition of RCDs and OCDs, the peak division in high-resolution XPS spectrum of each element was performed through a Thermo Avantage Surface Chemical Analysis Software. As shown in Fig. 1e, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra can be divided into three peaks, attributed to C-C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N/C-S (286.7 eV) and C = O (288.9 eV), The ratio of C = O groups in OCDs (35.49 %) is slightly higher than that in RCDs (34.61 %). The phenomenon was also verified by the O 1s spectra (Fig. 1f), where the O 1 s spectra showed two peaks at 532.1 eV and 533.3 eV, corresponding to O = C and O-C, respectively. OCDs possessed a ratio of 49.16 % for O = C groups, whereas RCDs has a relatively lower ratio of 42.56 %. The above results together reveal that OCDs have a higher oxidation state for the introduce of APS, which acted as an oxidant. As shown in Fig. 1g, the two peaks in high-resolution N 1 s could be decomposed into C-N (399.8 eV), and N-H (401.5 eV) groups, respectively. It is worth noting that the quantity of C-N groups in OCDs (57.93 %) is much more than that of RCDs (29.04 %). It could be speculated that the high concentration of ammonium ions in APS was conducive to the formation of C-N bonds in the synthesis of CDs. Furthermore, the high-resolution S 2p spectra disclosed the existence of the S-C/S-N (168.0 eV) and S = O (169.1 eV) in both CDs (Fig. 1h) [28].

Over all, the results of XPS were consistent with FTIR, indicating that there are multiple functional groups on the surface of CDs, ensuring their water solubility and providing interaction sites. In addition, there may be a difference in the proportion of functional groups on the two CDs as a result of the introduction of APS. A detailed study of the photoluminescence of OCDs and RCDs was conducted and the results were displayed in Fig. S4a–b. The significant differences in the fluorescence properties of the two types of carbon dots are primarily reflected in two aspects. On one hand, the excitation and emission wavelengths of OCDs (290 nm and 360 nm) are significantly blue-shifted compared to RCD (350 nm and 470 nm). The incorporation of heteroatom groups, such as nitrogen and sulfur, can function as electron traps or energy transfer channels, thereby influencing the recombination process of electrons and holes and regulating the fluorescent excitation and emission wavelengths. Given the complex photoluminescence mechanisms of CDs, which arise from a multitude of factors, it can be speculated that the differing proportions of N-containing functional groups in two CDs may lead to distinct wavelengths. OCDs possess a higher proportion of C-N groups, and the high electron density associated with nitrogen atoms tends to attract more electrons and stabilize the negative charge state. This may lead to a broadening of the band gap, consequently causing a blue shift in the excitation wavelength, which in turn leads to a blue shift in the emission wavelength [15,34,35]. On the other hand, OCDs exhibit excitation-independent emission properties, whereas RCDs possess excitation-dependent emission characteristics. As previously discussed, the preparation of OCDs involves doping with an oxidant (APS), which introduces additional oxygen-containing groups. As illustrated in Fig. 2 f, the ratio of C = O in OCDs is significantly enhanced compared to that in RCDs, thereby playing a crucial role in the luminescence behavior of OCDs. Consequently, they demonstrate photoluminescence that is independent of excitation. In contrast, RCDs may display a more balanced distribution of various functional groups, leading to multiple luminescence sites that exhibit excitation dependence [36]. Overall, the differences in elemental composition ratios and the relative proportions of functional groups between the two types of CDs account for the variations in their luminescence properties.

The fluorescence stability of CDs was studied in detail. As shown in Fig. S4c, fluorescence intensities of both RCDs and OCDs were stable across a wide pH range, from 3 to 10, and only slightly decreased under strong acidic or basic conditions. Fluorescence stability of OCDs was better under acidic conditions than RCDs, whereas it was the opposite for alkaline conditions. It is possible that pH can alter the structure of fluorescence luminescent groups on CDs by ionizing or protonating them, thereby changing the luminescence intensity [34,35]. Accordingly, there may be more proton-accepting groups (C-N) on the surface of OCDs, while more proton-giving groups (O-H/COOH) on RCDs, which was in agreement with the characterization results. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S4d, after long-term continuous irradiation with ultraviolet, both of the CDs displayed high fluorescence stability and exhibited their potential in the construction of sensors.

A two-channel sensor array was constructed in this work, using quenching efficiency (F0/F) of RCDs and OCDs triggered by TCs as the signal readout. The pH of the sensing system may affect the existing forms of both target analytes and functional groups on CDs, thus altering their interaction [37]. As shown in Fig. S5a, the effects of pH on the quenching efficiency of RCDs/OCDs by different TCs were quite different. Considering the simplicity of operation, pH 7 was utilized in the following experiments. As depicted in Fig. S5b, the fluorescence of RCDs and OCDs can be quenched quickly and remained constant thereafter. Hence, 5 min was sufficient for the system to reach equilibrium and selected as the incubation duration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and discernibility of the sensor array to TCs
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal sensing conditions, the selectivity and discernibility of the constructed sensor array were evaluated. TCs and various potential interfering substances, including other types of antibiotics, ions, and amino acids, were incubated with each sensing unit at a concentration of 10 μg mL−1. The fluorescence spectra are depicted in Fig. 2a–b, while the fluorescence intensity of the systems was measured, and the quenching efficiency was calculated and displayed in Fig. 2c. It can be concluded that only TCs showed a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence of RCDs and OCDs, indicating the high specificity of this sensor array to TCs. Moreover, the fluorescence of RCDs or OCDs could be quenched by five different TCs to varying degrees. Specifically, with F0/F as the readout signal the dual-channel response pattern of the sensor array for the five TCs was illustrated as a “fingerprint” map with unique signals readout for each (Fig. 2c with shadow). Further, the heat map intuitively exhibited the distinctive fluorescence response patterns for TCs (Fig. 2d), with the high and low colors representing fluorescence quenching efficiency. Then, a fluorescence response matrix consisting of 2 CDs × 5 TCs × 7 repetitions were built. LDA, a linear classifier especially well-suited for chemical sensors, was selected for the purpose of reducing data dimensions. Comparing with HCA and PCA, LDA is particularly appropriate for analyzing low-dimensional datasets, where the homoscedasticity assumption is largely valid and the number of sample replicates typically outnumbers the chosen independent dimensions. Moreover, LDA is easily defensible: the criteria used for maximizing the construction of discriminant dimensions and the mathematical techniques applied to realize these criteria are both well-established and analytically solvable [20]. By reducing the training matrix size through LDA, the raw data were transformed into canonical factors, thus converting complex multidimensional data into simple visualization patterns. It can be seen from the 2D canonical score plots that the data were distinctly clustered into five individual groups at concentration ranges of 0.5 ∼ 50 μg mL−1, with no overlap among the 95 % confidence ellipses (Fig. 2e–g). These results demonstrate that the constructed dual-channel sensor array can distinguish the TCs accurately.

In order to evaluate the quantitative analytical performance, the fluorescence responses of the sensor array to TCs at different concentrations were measured and LDA was also performed. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a–e, the LDA plots for TCs with various concentrations showed different clusters without overlap, indicating the superior discrimination capability of the sensor array for TCs of different concentrations. Further, since the first discriminant factor (factor 1) accounted for more than 97 % of the total variability, it was used as an output value for quantifying TCs. By plotting factor 1 (y) against analyte concentration (x), standard curves were constructed for the five TCs (Fig. 3f–j). The linearity correlation coefficients of the standard curves were all greater than 97 %, demonstrating that the sensor array could detect TCs quantitatively. The calculated limits of detection (LODs) were 0.31 μM for CTC, 0.70 μM for DOX, 0.22 μM for OTC, 0.25 μM for TET and 0.22 μM for TGC, respectively.

The discrimination ability of the sensor array was firstly tested to distinguish binary, ternary and quaternary mixtures with of TCs with identical percentages. As shown in the LDA plot (Fig. 4 a–c), 10 kinds of binary mixtures (1:1), 6 kinds of ternary mixtures (1:1:1) and 5 kinds quaternary mixtures (1:1:1:1) were clustered into individual groups without any overlap, demonstrating the mixtures could be distinguished precisely from each other by the sensor array. Moreover, three combinations of binary mixtures (OTC and TET, CTC and OTC, TET and DOX) were chosen as representatives to test whether the sensor array can distinguish binary mixtures of TCs at different ratios. As disclosed in Fig. 4d–f, there was good discrimination performance for binary mixture of TCs at six different ratios (0:5; 1:4; 2:3; 3:2; 4:1; 5:0). Overall, the sensor array showed excellent abilities to distinguish the mixtures of TCs comparing with other reports listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination and quantification of TCs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor array was adopted to detect TCs in tap water, lake water and milk samples and no TCs was found. To further confirm the applicability, individual TCs were spiked into these samples respectively and analyzed through the sensor array. After processed by the LDA, the corresponding responses of sensor array towards five kinds of TCs samples can be separated into five individual clusters (Fig. 4g–i). The recovery at the spiked concentration of high, middle, and low levels ranged from 85.1 % to 114.4 % (Table S1), with a relative standard deviation (RSD) lower than 8.1 %. the results of blind samples tests exhibited an accuracy of 100 % as revealed in Table S2. Overall, the developed sensor array was proved to be a viable strategy for identifying and quantifying TCs in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the sensor array for identification of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S6 showed that the absorption spectrum of the mixture of OCDs and individual TCs was almost a sum of the absorption spectra of the OCDs and TCs, thus ruling out the possibility of a static quenching process. While, the absorption peak of DOX showed a red shift and the absorption peak of CTC and OTC demonstrated a blue shift after their mixing with RCDs, which indicated that stable complexes were formed, suggesting a static mechanism may be responsible for the process of quenching [24]. A comparison was done between the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RCDs/OCDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of TCs. According to Fig. 5 a, TCs had different absorbances between 200 and 450 nm, and the excitation bands of RCDs overlapped with them. As a result, competitive absorption of excitation light can quench the emission of RCDs effectively, suggesting the presence of an inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism for sensing TCs [26]. TCs demonstrated different absorbance at the excitation wavelength of RCDs, which may result in different fluorescence quenching efficiency. Unlike RCDs, both the excitation and emission bands of OCDs had large portions of overlap with the absorption spectra of TCs (Fig. 5b), which indicated fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) should also be possible quenching mechanisms [38]. Fluorescence lifetimes of RCDs/OCDs and their incubation with TCs were measured in Fig. 5c–d. The fluorescence decay curves were fitted well with the double exponential function and the average lifetimes were calculated. According to the results, except for OTC, the presence of TCs barely changed the lifetime of RCDs/OCDs. Fluorescence lifetimes of RCDs before and after addition of OTC were 5.57 ns and 4.42 ns, respectively. As a result of the addition of OTC, the fluorescence lifetime of OCDs was changed from 7.29 to 5.56 ns. Therefore, fluorescence quenching of RCDs/OCDs by OTC may proceed through a dynamic quenching mechanism. In view of the fact that except OTC, the fluorescence lifetime of OCDs remained unchanged after mixture with TCs, the FRET mechanism may only take place between OTC and OCDs [39].

To investigate the interaction mode and strength of different tetracyclines with the CDs, molecular docking was initially employed. The 3D structures of all small molecules for docking were constructed from Chem3D 14.0 and energy minimization was performed under MMFF94 force field. Literature indicated that CDs were composed of carbon nuclei and surface functional groups [40]. Graphite carbon core was built using the Charmm-gui (https://charmm-gui.org/). Considering the fact that the surface functional groups of CDs were commonly inherited from carbon source molecules, carbon source molecules were directly chosen as the surface functional groups for computer modeling of CDs [41,42]. Firstly, the docking box was set to wrap around the surface of the graphite carbon core. PyMol 2.5.5 software was used to combine molecules with graphite carbon core to form RCDs and OCDs. Based on the computer-modeled RCDs and OCDs, the binding affinity between RCDs/OCDs and TCs was studied with a binding box wrapping around the surface of RCDs/OCDs. Docking results were visualized with PyMol 2.5.5. As observed in Fig. 5e–f and Fig. S7, there may be a wide variety of interactions between TCs and functional groups, i.e. the π-π interaction, the hydrogen bonding interaction, and the cation-π interaction. When citric acid and DSS were applied for preparation of RCDs, besides hydrogen bonding interaction, the cation-π interaction existed between RCDs and the majority of TCs. When APS was introduced into the CDs preparation system, there was no longer a cation-π interaction but stronger hydrogen bonding. Docking scores of the interaction between RCDs/OCDs and TCs were shown in Table S3. Different docking scores indicated different interactions between them. The lower the docking score, the stronger the binding energy. According to the results, there was a difference in the mode and strength of interactions between RCDs and OCDs with TCs. Since the order of quenching efficiency was not the same as that of one particular parameter (spectral overlap rate, binding energy), the fluorescence quenching of CDs by TCs may occur through a variety of mechanisms. In summary, the IFE was most likely to dominate the fluorescence response of CDs to TCs. In addition, there may be static quenching between RCD and DOX/CTC. Fluorescence quenching of RCDs by OTC may be caused by static quenching, dynamic quenching and internal filtering. OTC induced fluorescence quenching of OCDs possibly through FRET, dynamic quenching and internal filtering effects. Due to the different quenching degrees, TCs discrimination were achieved by the sensor array. In comparison with the previous reports, the study displays a superiority of in-depth mechanism exploration.

In this study, two distinct CDs were synthesized successfully by regulating the doping concentration of the oxidant (APS) with identical carbon source. A two-channel sensor array for TCs discrimination was constructed with the two CDs as sensing elements, based on their significant differences in the surface functional groups composition and fluorescence response behaviors to TCs through a combination of various quenching mechanisms. The TCs alone or in mixture can produce unique fluorescence response patterns and be differentiated accurately through LDA. In addition, the sensor array demonstrated favorable quantification capability for five homologous TCs in concentration ranges of 0.5 ∼ 50 μg mL−1. The sensor array was successfully applied in the identification and detection of TCs in tap water, lake water and milk with high recovery rates of 85.1 ∼ 114.4 %. Molecular docking was successfully employed to investigate the interaction mode and strength of different tetracyclines with the CDs. In general, this work provided an ideal platform for identification and measurement of TCs in environmental and food samples.

Jing Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Su Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Ya-Lan Duan: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yan-Ting Song: Writing – review & editing, Software, Writing – original draft. Lu-Shuang Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112372.
